coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanofiltration membranes towards efficient biogas slurry valorization
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Wen-Jie
Shao, Dan-Dan
Zhou, Zhengzhong
Xia, Qian-Cheng
Chen, Jian
Cao, Xue-Li
Zheng, Tao
Sun, Shi-Peng
coredata.dc:description: In this article, novel nanofiltration (NF) membranes with highly selective surface and fast water and ion transport channels were fabricated for the valorization of biogas slurry. Unlike conventional thin film composite (TFC) membranes, the novel NF membranes compose hydrophilic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the interlayer. The carboxyl groups from CQDs react with the polyethyleneimine (PEI), leading to the lower cross-linking degree in the interfacial polymerization layer of NF membranes. Besides, the presence of the hydrophilic CQDs can create fast water and ion transport channels in the NF membranes. The water permeability of membranes is therefore enhanced significantly from 3.6 to 9.7 LMH·bar−1. The NF membranes were used to concentrate the amino acids from biogas slurry; it achieved rejection rates above 90% towards model amino acids such as lysine, glutamic acid and leucine, due to its highly positive electrical properties, while allowing most of the unwanted salt ions to permeate through. The excellent selectivity between amino acids and salt ions demonstrates the great potential application of the NF membranes in biogas slurry valorization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Biogas slurry
Amino acids
Thin-film composite (TFC)
Nanofiltration

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology of CQDs and CQDs based membranes
Effects of CQDs on the structure, wettability and transport properties of the membranes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface and cross-section SEM images of membrane based on the different CQDs concentration.
XRD spectrum of TFC membrane and CQDs/TFC membranes based on different CQDs concentration.

XPS spectrum of different membranes: (a) wide scan spectrum status of substrate (b) N1s elemental analysis of 2%CQDs–PEI/PES substrate; (c) and (d) N1s element analysis of TFC membrane and CQDs/TFC membrane respectively.
(a) Pore size distribution curves and (b) probability density function curves of TFC membrane and CQDs/TFC membranes based on different PEI molecular weight.
(a) Dynamic water contact angle and (b) drop CD of TFC membrane and CQDs/TFC membranes based on different CQDs concentration.
Influence of CQDs on properties of TFC membrane based on different PEI molecular weight: (a) pure water permeation; (b) the rejection to MgCl2 (1000 ppm) under pressure of 6 bar.
The mechanism of fast ions transport channels.
Pure water permeability and the rejection to lysine for TFC and CQDs/TFC membrane based on (a) different PEI concentration; (b) different CQDs concentration.
Rejection of three amino acids under different pH value of CQDs/TFC membrane.
Zeta potential curves of CQDs/TFC membrane, DL and DK commercial membrane.
Separation performance between mixed amino acids and negative ions of CQDs/TFC membrane. (a) Under different pH; (b) comparison of CQDs/TFC membrane, DL and DK membrane.
(a) Permeability change; (b) Rejection and (c) separation factor at the beginning and end of the concentration process of CQDs/TFC membrane, DL and DK membrane.
Preparation process of CQDs/TFC membrane and separation mechanism between amino acids and salt ions.
The chemical composition of different membranes derived from XPS spectra.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a Molar ratio of nitrogen to oxygen in different membrane.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of properties between membrane in this work and commercial membrane and previous reported.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanofiltration membranes towards efficient biogas slurry valorization

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: W.J. Yang and D.D. Shao contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this article, novel nanofiltration (NF) membranes with highly selective surface and fast water and ion transport channels were fabricated for the valorization of biogas slurry. Unlike conventional thin film composite (TFC) membranes, the novel NF membranes compose hydrophilic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the interlayer. The carboxyl groups from CQDs react with the polyethyleneimine (PEI), leading to the lower cross-linking degree in the interfacial polymerization layer of NF membranes. Besides, the presence of the hydrophilic CQDs can create fast water and ion transport channels in the NF membranes. The water permeability of membranes is therefore enhanced significantly from 3.6 to 9.7 LMH·bar−1. The NF membranes were used to concentrate the amino acids from biogas slurry; it achieved rejection rates above 90% towards model amino acids such as lysine, glutamic acid and leucine, due to its highly positive electrical properties, while allowing most of the unwanted salt ions to permeate through. The excellent selectivity between amino acids and salt ions demonstrates the great potential application of the NF membranes in biogas slurry valorization.

ce:section.ce:para: Biogas slurry is the anaerobic fermentation residue of various organic substances such as human, livestock and poultry manure and crop straw [1]. The biogas slurry is rich in nutrients, which can be utilized as green fertilizers for agricultural applications. It contains various amino acids such as lysine, leucine and glutamic acid, which are valuable raw materials for amino acids-containing liquid fertilizer. However, biogas slurry is often large in quantity and costly to store and transport, which limits its application as fertilizer for direct irrigation as fertilizer. As a result, a large amount of biogas slurry is discharged directly, bringing not only threat to the environment and human health, but also a serious waste of resources [2].

Membrane technology has been widely used in various fields, such as organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) [3], proton exchanging [4], pervaporation dehydration [5], wastewater treatment [6,7] and so on. Membrane concentration technology provides a new approach for the utilization of biogas slurry resources. The volume of the concentrate can be significantly reduced in comparison to the original raw materials while the concentration of nutrients is increased. The concentrated biogas slurry is convenient for storage, transportation and further processing to become high value-added liquid fertilizer.

At present, the biogas slurry is mainly treated with reverse osmosis (RO) membranes [1,8]. Not only the environmental pollution caused by direct discharge of slurry is solved, the permeated water from the RO unit can also be reused to save utility cost. However, two important issues are still faced by RO technology in the above application: First, the water permeability of RO membranes is relatively low, which requires high pressure to drive the concentration process, thereby increases the energy consumption. Second, the biogas slurry contains a large amount of inorganic salts, which leads to not only concentration polarization effects, but also high osmotic pressure, thus reducing the effective driving force during the RO process. The lifetime and concentration efficiency of the reverse osmosis membranes will severely deteriorate.

Being a new membrane separation technology, nanofiltration (NF) has been developed rapidly since the late 1980s with the molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) between 200 and 2000. The mean pore size is in the range of 0.5–2.0 nm, which is between RO and ultrafiltration (UF) membranes. The separation mechanisms of NF membranes include pore size screening and Donnan effect. Compared to RO membranes, NF membranes have two advantages in biogas slurry valorization: (1) higher flux; (2) more importantly, NF can be used for not only rejection but also separation [9–15]. In recent years, there are growing interests to study the retention and separation mechanisms of amino acids by utilizing commercial NF membranes such as Dow NF270, Suez DK, DL and HL. As zwitterions, pH was found to be factor important in determining the retention of amino acids [16,17]. Researchers have also put extensive efforts in improving flux and selectivity of typical amino acids through manipulating membrane topology or surface characteristics with polyelectrolyte nanoparticles and other methods [18,19].

Nevertheless, the development of suitable NF membranes for biogas slurry valorization is still a challenging topic. The major problem is that the biogas slurry contains a large amount of small molecular-weight amino acids (<200 g·mol−1) as well as negative ions (SO4 2−, Cl−). Current NF membranes suffer from two issues: (1) Most existing NF membranes utilized in separation of amino acids are negatively charged, which are ineffective in permeating SO4 2−, Cl−, according to Donnan exclusion. This may weaken the advantage of NF over RO [20–22]. (2) Due to the small molecular weight of amino acids, tight NF membranes are required for higher retention with the sacrifice of water flux. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a novel NF membrane specially tailored for separating amino acids and inorganic salts with high selectivity as well as high flux.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are zero-dimensional carbon-based materials synthesized by an environmental-friendly one-pot aqueous thermal process, has attracted tremendous attention around the world in recent years [23–27]. Superior to other carbon-based small nanoparticles, CQDs has a smaller average size of less than 10 nm. In addition, CQDs has uniform dispersion in water due to the plenty of hydrophilic carbonyl and carboxyl groups, such that they can be conveniently processed during membrane fabrication and functionalization for performance enhancement [28–31].

Therefore, the main objective of the current work is to develop a novel strategy to reconstruct the structure of thin-film composite (TFC) NF membranes with CQDs for rapid and precise separation of amino acids and inorganic ions. The detailed strategy, which is shown in Scheme 1 , is as following: (1) An interlayer containing CQDs is inserted between the substrate and selective layers of conventional TFC membranes. The new conformation may boost the formation of transport channels for flux enhancement. (2) The surface charge of the interfacial polymerized selective layer is finely tuned with polyethyleneimine (PEI) for improving the selectivity between amino acids and inorganic ions. This work may provide insightful guidelines to develop next-generation NF membranes for rapid and precise separation of small molecules for biogas slurry valorization and other industries.

Polyethersulfone (PES) ultrafiltration membranes were employed as substrates and supplied by Nanjing Industrial Technology Research Institute of Membranes Co. Ltd; the membranes had a pure water permeability (PWP) of 350 L·h−1·m−2·bar−1 and a mean pore size of 22.0 nm. d-(+)-glucose (AR, Merck) was used to synthesize functionalized CQDs. NaOH (AR) and 2-choro-1-methylpyridinium iodide (CMPI, 98%) for activating carboxyl groups of CQDs were purchased from Shanghai Macldin Biochemical Co. Ltd and Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd respectively. Polyethyleneimine (PEI, >99%, Aladdin) with molecular weight 1800, 10 000, 70 000, trimesoyl chloride (TMC, 99%, Aladdin) and n-hexane (>97.0%, Aladdin) were used for interfacial polymerization. Polyethylene glycols (PEGs, MW = 200, 400, 600, 1000) were purchased from Merck to characterize the molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of the NF membranes. Lysine (98%), leucine (chromatographic purity) and glutamic acid (chromatographic purity) were purchased from Aladdin and China Huixing Biochemical Reagent Co. Ltd. The detailed properties of the amino acids are shown in Table S1.

CQDs were synthesized according to a hydrothermal approach according to the previously work in our group [32]. First, a pre-determined amount of 3 g glucose and 8 g PEG400 were dissolved in 24 ml DI water and stirred for a certain time under room temperature. Then the mixture was transferred into a 50 ml steel reactor and heated at 180 °C for 20 h. After cooling down to room temperature, a CQD solution was obtained without any further purification.

The preparation process of CQDs/TFC membrane is illustrated in Scheme 1. First, the PES substrate was immersed in an aqueous solution containing 0.1 g CMPI, 0.1 g NaOH, 1 g CQDs and 48.8 g deionized water for 10 min to activate the –COOH groups [33]. The modified substrate was named as “CQDs/PES”. After removing the residual solution, a polyamide layer was formed on the CQDs/PES substrate through interfacial polymerization between PEI and TMC. Specifically, the modified PES substrates were submergedin a 2 wt% homogeneous PEI solution for two minutes. After removing the excess PEI by a rubber roller, the membranes were then exposed to a hexane solution containing 0.1 wt% TMC for 1 min. The unreacted reagents were removed with DI water. The resulted membrane was named as “CQDs/TFC” membrane and stored in DI water before characterization.

For comparison, the unmodified PES substrates were used to fabricate NF membranes, the selective layer of which was synthesized from pure PEI with different molecular weight, defined as “TFC” membrane. Besides, a “PEI/CQDs/PES” membrane was also prepared by first immersing a substrate modified by 2% CQDs into PEI solution, followed by removing the residual PEI and thoroughly washes by pure water.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM 2100, Japan) was used to measure the size of CQDs. The test condition was 200 kV accelerating voltage while the CQDs were dissolved in water in advance.

The membrane morphology was observed by a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Hitachi SU4800, Japan). The membranes were coated with gold nanoparticles by a sputter coater to enhance the conductivity of membrane surface. A SurPASS3 electrokinetic analyzer (Anton Paar, Austria) was used to measure the membrane surface charge with 1 mM KCl solution circulated through the measuring cell. The pH was adjusted with 0.1 M HCl and 0.1 M NaOH solution during the test. Dynamic water contact angle (Kruss DSA CA goniometer, Germany) was used to examine the membrane surface wettability. The surface roughness of membranes was observed by atomic force microscopy (AFM, XE-100, Korea). The membranes were freeze-dried before test and the test size is 0.3 μm × 0.3 μm. X-Ray diffraction (XRD) (Miniflex 600) was used to investigate the crystal degree of membrane. The testing angle was from 5° to 60°. Before characterization, each membrane was freeze dried overnight in order to protect the pore structure from collapsing.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Escalab250Xi, Thermo Scientific) was applied for examining the content of elements of the polyamide layer and the substrate. The cross-linking degree (D) of interfacial polymerization could be calculated as the Eq. (1):
(1)
D
( % )
=
m
m + n
× 100
where m and n stand for the cross-linked and linear parts of the selective layer respectively [34]. The value of m and n could be obtained from the Eqs. (2) and (3) by O/N ratio:
(2)
m + n = 1
(3)
O N
=
3 m + 4 n
3 m + 2 n

A dead-end permeation cell with constant stirring was applied to measure the pure water permeability and the solute rejection under 6 bar. The effective filtration area of the membrane was approximately 12.56 cm2. Thus the pure water permeability (PWP, L·m−2·h−1·bar−1) was calculated using the followed equations:
(4)
P W P =
Q
Δ P ∙
A m
where Q represents the water permeation volumetric flow rate (L/h), A m stands for the effective filtration area (m2), and ΔP is the trans-membrane pressure difference (bar).

The solute rejection was determined by the followed equation:
(5)
R
%
=
1 -
c p
c f
× 100 %
where c p and c f are the solute concentrations in the permeate and the feed solution, respectively. The salt concentration used in the test is 1000 ppm. The concentration of salt and amino acids were obtained from a conductivity meter (FE30, Mettler-Toledo, USA) and a total organic carbon analyzer (TOC-VCPH Shimadzu) respectively. Each membrane was filtrated with distilled water overnight to remove any residual solvent and pre-passed for half an hour in order to stabilize the performance of the film.

The separation and concentration tests of mixed amino acids and salts were carried out in a dead-end set up at 6 bar with different pH. The mixture was prepared according to the biogas slurry composition from an anaerobic digestion (AD) company in Jieyang (China); the concentrations of three model amino acids (lysine, glutamic acid and leucine) and two model inorganic salts (Na2SO4, NaCl) were 200 ppm, 200 ppm, 400 ppm, 400 ppm and 300 ppm, respectively. The concentration of SO4 2− and Cl− were tested by ion chromatography (ICS 2000, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The separation factor (S) was calculated with the following formula.
(6)
S =
R A
R S
where R A and R s represent the rejection to amino acids and ions respectively.

PEG 200, PEG 400, PEG 600 and PEG 1000 were applied to determine the molecular weight cutoff (MWCO), mean effective pore size and pore size distribution of the NF membranes. The solute concentration and operating pressure were kept at 1000 ppm and 6 bar, respectively. The solute radius r s of PEG with different molecular weights were calculated by the following equation:
(7)
r S
= 16.73 ×
10
- 12
×
M
0.557
where M is the molecular weight of different PEGs. The pore size distribution of the membrane was calculated by the following formula [35]:
(8)
d
R T

)

l n

2 π

2
where r p is the mean effective pore radius found at R T = 55.0% when a straight line of solute rejection R T vs. r s is plotted on log-normal probability coordinates. The geometric standard deviation σ p is the ratio of r s at R T = 84.13% and 50%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology of CQDs and CQDs based membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The as prepared CQDs dispersed uniformly in water. Fig. S1(a) and (b) show no obvious aggregate of solutes after the solution was standing for one week. As suggested by TEM observation in Fig. S1(c), spherical dots-like CQDs are distributed uniformly with an average diameter around 6 nm. This is mostly due to the existence of hydrophilic –COOH and –OH groups derived from CQDs that assist the dissolution process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CQDs on the structure, wettability and transport properties of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
XPS was used to investigate the chemical structure of membranes. The exact element contents of C, N, O and S of substrate and PA membrane are presented in Table 1 . The disappearance of sulfur element in the TFC and CQDs/TFC membrane proves the successful deposition of the interfacial polymerized layer on the substrate. It can be observed that the ratio of N/O in the CQDs/TFC films is lower than that of the control TFC membrane, indicating that the cross-linking degree of PA layer is decreased (ranging from 62.7% to 50.7%). The reasons are as follows:
1.
Fig. 3 (a) shows the increase of the N1s peak intensity at 400 eV binding energy of the 2%CQDs-PEI/PES membrane as compared to the pristine PES membrane; the N atomic content increases from 0.42% to 7.29% as shown in Table 1. In addition, Fig. 3(b) also shows the peaks of the di- or tertiary amine, meaning some PEI still exist on the surface of membrane after rinse by water. The binding force may derive from the reaction between part of the amino groups in PEI and carbonyl groups on the carbon quantum dot which could reduce the degree of cross-linking of the PA layer and build fast ions transport channels. It should be noted that the nitrogen content of the 2% CQDs/PES membrane also increased because the activator CMPI has nitrogen, which increases the nitrogen content.
2.
The negatively charged –OH and –COOH groups of CQDs could slow down the migration rate of PEI into the TMC phase during interfacial polymerization via electrostatic attraction, thus decreases the cross-linking degree during interfacial polymerization. This interpretation is corroborated by the XPS analysis results as shown in Fig. 3(c) and (d): the –NH2 groups content is higher than that of pure PEI/TMC membrane, suggesting more unreacted –NH2 groups, hence lower the cross-linking degree in the CQDs/TFC membrane.

Lower cross-linking degree of PA layer may lead to larger pore size. The cumulative pore size distribution curves and probability density function curves of the membranes with different molecular weight of PEI are derived from Eqs. (7) and (8) and shown in Fig. 4 (a) and (b), respectively. The MWCO of these membranes are shown in Table S2. It can be observed that the pore size and the MWCO of CQDs/TFC-PEI 70 K membrane are larger than those of TFC-PEI 70 K membrane, which is attributed to the decreased cross-linking degree, as proved in earlier section. Interestingly, by lowering the PEI molecular weight, the pore size of CQDs/TFC gradually decrease. The MWCO of CQDs/TFC-PEI 1.8 K is quite similar to the pristine PEI membrane. The smaller MWCO associated with smaller PEI molecular weight is mainly due to the higher density of functional group available for cross-linking in smaller PEI [38]. Thus, we may expect tunable pore size and flux by simply varying PEI molecular weight.

Dynamic water contact angle (WCA) measurement was performed to evaluate the surface wettability of the membranes prepared with different concentration of CQDs. Fig. 5 (a) indicates that the initial water contact angle decreases with increasing CQDs concentration. All the WCA of CQDs/TFC membrane decrease more quickly with time compared to the TFC membrane. Fig. 5(b) shows that all drop-conjugated diameter (drop CD) have not significantly changed. These phenomena can be explained by two reasons: (1) The interfacial polymerized layer has a lower cross-linking degree due to the embedding of CQDs, which could facilitate the transport of water molecules through the larger pores. (2) The CQDs interlayer has a positive effect on dragging water molecules to pass through the PA layer due to the hydrophilic group of the CQDs and fast ions transport channel [39,40]. The findings suggest that the water transfer mechanism is the penetration process, according to the previously reported literature [39].

The permeability and retention experiments are carried out in a dead-end filtration set-up with 1000 ppm MgCl2 feed solution and the results are presented in Fig. 6 (a). It is obvious that the permeability of the CQDs modified PEI 70 K membrane has enhanced by a factor of 2.7, i.e. from 3.6 to 9.72 LMH·Bar−1. At the mean time, the membrane still shows a high retention of MgCl2 (95.3%) (Fig. 6(b)). The higher permeability without compromising the rejection rate demonstrates the positive impact of the CQDs, which could be illustrated by the following reasons.
1.
The lower cross-linking degree causes the larger pore size in PA layer. So that the water molecules have more free volume to pass through the membrane.
2.
As shown in Fig. 7 , the conventional TFC membrane penetrates into the pores of the substrate during the process of interfacial polymerization. In this case, the distance that the water and ions permeate through the membrane becomes longer, which will increase the mass transfer resistance. With the introduction of CQDs as the intermediate layer, transmission of water molecules will accelerate because: (1) the hydrophilicity is enhanced; (2) the selective layer is deposited directly on the surface of the CQDs, which reduces the mass transfer resistance of the water molecules and increases the flux of both water and ions.

It is interesting that the membrane prepared with larger PEI molecule (70 K) has the largest enhancement compared with those with smaller molecular weight (1.8 K and 10 K). The reason may be the lower cross-linking degree associated with the 70 K PEI during interfacial polymerization than those with smaller PEI molecules as discussed in the previous section. The larger free volume in the interfacial polymeric layer intensifies the influence of CQDs in dragging water molecules through the membrane.

In order to explore the best membrane performance, the impact of two important parameters during the NF membrane fabrication is investigated. The CQDs concentration ranging from 2% to 6% and PEI concentration ranging from 1% to 3% were chosen to fabricate various NF membranes. The membrane performance was then characterized with a lysine (200 ppm) solution. Fig. 8 (a) shows higher permeability of all modified membranes in comparison to the unmodified membranes. The permeability of CQDs/TFC membrane is the highest at 1% PEI concentration and then decreases with further increment in PEI concentration. The reason is that higher PEI concentrations lead to thicker polyamide layers, resulting in lower water fluxes. However, one should also note that a thicker polyamide layer is beneficial for solute retention, as evidenced from the slightly better rejection of lysine. A similar trend is also observed in the effects of carbon quantum dots concentration on membrane performance (Fig. 8(b)). Interestingly, increasing CQDs concentration results in a reduction of water permeability. The drop in permeability could be explained by the crystal appeared on the surface of membrane at too high CQDs concentration. As the crystal may have no water transport channels, a large amount of crystals occupy the free volume of the NF membrane skin layer. Therefore, the water permeability of the NF membrane is reduced and the flux is lower [36,37,41].

Apart from the amino acids, biogas slurry also contained inorganic ions such as SO4 2− and Cl−. The retention of these negatively charged ions causes the following potential problems: first, the osmotic pressure of the feed biogas slurry would increase with operation time, reducing the effective driving force across the membrane, so that the permeation flux, and the membrane performance may deteriorate; second, concentration polarization becomes more serious and again decreases the effective flux; third, the presence of inorganic salts may affect the quality of the end product, i.e. the liquid fertilizer containing amino acids, as excessive salts could damage the soil environment. In view of the above issues, the inorganic ions should be retained as less as possible by the NF membrane.

Fig. S6 shows the salt rejection of the CQDs/TFC membrane in a decreasing order: MgCl2 > MgSO4 > Na2SO4 > NaCl. According to the Donnan exclusion theory, a strong electrostatic repulsion between Mg2+ and the positively charged membrane surface exists.[43,44] Thus the rejection of MgCl2 is the highest among all the four salts. The rejection of MgSO4 and Na2SO4 are much lower than MgCl2, because the effective charge density on the membrane surface could be decreased due to the adsorption of SO4 2− [18]. Cl− ion has the lowest rejection as it has the smallest Stokes radius (Table S3) and lower ionic valence. It could pass more easily through the relatively looser and more hydrophilic CQDs/TFC membrane [45].

The filtration experiments using a model mixture containing amino acids and inorganic ions at different pH values were carried out. As shown in Fig. 11 (a), the rejections of sulfate and chloride ions are lower than single salt rejection. This may be due to the competitive effect of the negatively charged ions. During the filtration process, negatively charged ions would be attracted by the positively charged membrane to preferentially pass through the membrane compared to the ions with opposite charges [46]. The best separation is observed when pH is adjusted to 3, and the rejection rates follow the order: amino acids > SO4 2− > Cl−. At higher pH conditions, the membrane surface charge becomes negative, such that the rejection of SO4 2− and Cl− is increased. Besides, the separation factor between amino acids and mixed ions shows the highest value of 2.45 at pH = 3, suggesting that the dominant separation mechanism is Donna exclusion. This result above also indicates the feasibility of using a positively charged membrane to separate amino acids from ions.

The commercial potential of the membrane prepared in this study for biogas slurry application was investigated by comparing the filtration performance against commercial DK and DL membranes at pH 3. As shown in Fig. 11(b), both the rejections of SO4 2− and Cl− by the negatively charged DK and DL membrane are higher than that of the CQDs/TFC membrane. The DK and DL membrane is almost electrical neutral (Fig. 10) at pH 3, which is not easy for negatively charged ions to pass through. The rejection of amino acids is also lower in the commercial DK and DL membrane, due to the lack of electrostatic repulsion between amino acids and the membrane surface. As a result, the separation factors of commercial membranes are much lower than the CQDs/TFC membrane.

The concentration performance of the CQDs/TFC membrane and the commercial membranes were compared by enriching the simulated biogas slurry model solution by 10 times, i.e. reducing the amino acids and salt solution volume from 500 ml to 50 ml in a dead-end filtration cell at pH 3. Fig. 12 (a) shows that the high permeability of the CQDs/TFC membrane is maintained and reaches a plateau while the permeability decline is clearly observed for the commercial membranes. This is because the salt concentration increase concentration polarization and builds up the osmotic pressure, thus lowering the effective driving force and consequently the water permeability, which also proves that the NF membranes for biogas slurry valorization should be specially designed rather than using the commercial membranes for general applications.

Fig. 12(b) shows that, at the final stage of the concentration process, the ion rejections of the DL and DK commercial membranes decrease by 6.7% and 15.2%, respectively, while the amino acids rejection decrease by 13.4% and 13%, respectively, in comparison to the initial state. This is expected since the solute concentration at the membrane surface increases during the filtration process, the increase in solute concentration gradients drive more solutes to permeate through the membranes, which is reflected by the drop in solute rejection. However, the retention by the CQDs/TFC film does not decrease by the end of the concentration experiment. Instead, the salt ion rejection is even slightly increased while the amino acids rejection is maintained. Although this leads to a slight reduction of the ion separation factor of the CQDs/TFC membrane (Fig. 12(c)), it still outperforms the commercial membranes whose separation factor is unchanged.

The performance of the CQDs/TFC membrane is compared with commercial and academia membranes in Table 2 . The water permeability of CQDs/TFC membrane is higher than most of the membrane including normal polyamide membranes, polymeric membranes and commercial membranes. Besides, the rejection of amino acids surpasses that of the commercial membranes. We have demonstrated that the enhancement of water flux is due to the low cross-linking degree of polyamide layer and the hydrophilicity of CQDs, which facilitates the fast permeation of water molecules through the membrane. The high rejection of amino acids is mainly resulted from the strong electrostatic repulsion of the membrane. Therefore, we conclude that the CQDs/TFC membrane has a great potential for the valorization of biogas slurry.

In this work, we have designed a novel NF membrane by (1) building a relative loose and positively charged surface for high rejection of amino acids and high permeation of inorganic salts; (2) the interlayer composing of CQDs is powerful in enhancing water permeation. The new NF membrane is advantageous over conventional RO membranes for the concentration of biogas slurry. The following conclusions can be illustrated.
1.
The presence of the CQDs could boost water transport channels of polyamide layer by decreasing the amount of –NH2 groups in PEI available for cross-linking, via the reaction between –COOH groups in CQDs and –NH2 groups in PEI. The hydrophilicity of CQDs also facilitates the water transport through the membrane. The above synergic effects result in the higher water flux while maintain the amino acids rejection.
2.
The CQDs/TFC membrane demonstrates amazing separation performance between positively charged amino acids and negatively charged ions. It could alleviate the concentration polarization faced by conventional membranes and enhance the purity of concentrated amino acids.
Above all, we have demonstrated that the CQDs/TFC membrane is a great candidate for biogas slurry utilization, that reduces the environmental pollution and enhances the economic profit.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the research funding provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21878146), Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Fund Outstanding Youth Project (BK20190037), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20160495), and the Science & Technology Project of Guangdong Province (2016A010105017, 2017B040404009). Dr. Shi-Peng Sun is supported by the program of “Thousand Talents Plan-Distinguished Young Professor”. Mr. Wen-Jie Yang appreciates Miss. Hui-Fang Xiao, Mr. Jin-Peng Lu and Mr. Chang-Hui Chu for valuable discussions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.123993.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
